### Kushan monetary system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kushan_Empire&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Kushan monetary system")]

Main article: [Kushan coinage](/wiki/Kushan_coinage "Kushan coinage")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Dalverzin_Tepe_gold_ingots.jpg/250px-Dalverzin_Tepe_gold_ingots.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dalverzin_Tepe_gold_ingots.jpg)

Kushan gold ingots, from the [Dalverzin Tepe](/wiki/Dalverzin_Tepe "Dalverzin Tepe") treasure, 1st century CE

The Kushans used gold ingots as part of their monetary system, as shown by the gold treasure discovered in 1972 in [Dalverzin Tepe](/wiki/Dalverzin_Tepe "Dalverzin Tepe").[[127]](#cite_note-SPL68-135) The main objects from the treasure were circular and parallelepipedic ingots, followed by various decorative objects and jewellery items.[[127]](#cite_note-SPL68-135) The circular ingots used to be progressively cut up as needed, depending on the amount required for a transaction.[[127]](#cite_note-SPL68-135) On the contrary, the parallelepipedic ingots were used to stock wealth in a not-divisible form; these ingots bear inscriptions in [Kharoshthi](/wiki/Kharoshthi "Kharoshthi") mentioning their weight and the god [Mitra](/wiki/Mitra "Mitra") (protector of contractual relations)[[127]](#cite_note-SPL68-135) These ingots are all attributed to the monetary system of the Kushan Empire.[[127]](#cite_note-SPL68-135)

The coinage of the Kushans was abundant and an important tool of propaganda in promoting each Kushan ruler.[[128]](#cite_note-Sen-136) One of the names for Kushan coins was *[Dinara](/wiki/Dinar "Dinar")*, which ultimately came from the Roman name *[Denarius](/wiki/Denarius "Denarius") [aureus](/wiki/Aureus "Aureus")*.[[128]](#cite_note-Sen-136)[[129]](#cite_note-137)[[130]](#cite_note-138) The coinage of the Kushans was copied as far as the [Kushano-Sasanians](/wiki/Kushano-Sasanians "Kushano-Sasanians") in the west, and the kingdom of [Samatata](/wiki/Samatata "Samatata") in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") to the east. The coinage of the [Gupta Empire](/wiki/Gupta_Empire "Gupta Empire") was also initially derived from the coinage of the Kushan Empire, adopting its weight standard, techniques and designs, following the conquests of [Samudragupta](/wiki/Samudragupta "Samudragupta") in the northwest.[[131]](#cite_note-139)[[132]](#cite_note-RM-140)[[133]](#cite_note-141) The imagery on Gupta coins then became more Indian in both style and subject matter compared to earlier dynasties, where Greco-Roman and Persian styles were mostly followed.[[132]](#cite_note-RM-140)[[134]](#cite_note-142)

It has long been suggested that the gold contained in Kushan coins was ultimately of Roman origin, and that Roman coins were imported as a consequence of trade and melted in India to mint Kushan coins. However, a recent [archaeometallurgical study](/wiki/Archaeometallurgy "Archaeometallurgy") of trace elements through proton activation analysis has shown that Kushan gold contains high concentrations of [platinum](/wiki/Platinum "Platinum") and [palladium](/wiki/Palladium "Palladium"), which rules out the hypothesis of a Roman provenance. To this day, the origin of Kushan gold remains unknown.[[135]](#cite_note-143)

## Contacts with Rome

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kushan_Empire&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Contacts with Rome")]

Main article: [Indo-Roman trade relations](/wiki/Indo-Roman_trade_relations "Indo-Roman trade relations")

Roman coinage among the Kushans

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/TrajanCoinAhinposhBuddhistMonasteryAfghanistan.jpg/250px-TrajanCoinAhinposhBuddhistMonasteryAfghanistan.jpg)](/wiki/File:TrajanCoinAhinposhBuddhistMonasteryAfghanistan.jpg)

Coin of the Roman Emperor [Trajan](/wiki/Trajan "Trajan"), found together with coins of Kanishka the Great at the [Ahin Posh](/wiki/Ahin_Posh "Ahin Posh") Monastery

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Kushan_ring_with_Septimus_Severus_and_Julia_Domna.jpg/250px-Kushan_ring_with_Septimus_Severus_and_Julia_Domna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kushan_ring_with_Septimus_Severus_and_Julia_Domna.jpg)

Kushan ring with inscription in the [Brahmi script](/wiki/Brahmi_script "Brahmi script"), with portraits of Roman rulers [Septimius Severus](/wiki/Septimius_Severus "Septimius Severus") and [Julia Domna](/wiki/Julia_Domna "Julia Domna")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Septimius_Severus._AD_193-211._Indian_imitation.jpg/330px-Septimius_Severus._AD_193-211._Indian_imitation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Septimius_Severus._AD_193-211._Indian_imitation.jpg)

Indian imitation of a coin of Septimius Severus. AD 193–211

Several Roman sources describe the visit of ambassadors from the Kings of Bactria and India during the 2nd century, probably referring to the Kushans.[[136]](#cite_note-RML131-144)

[Historia Augusta](/wiki/Historia_Augusta "Historia Augusta"), speaking of [Emperor Hadrian](/wiki/Hadrian "Hadrian") (117–138) tells:[[136]](#cite_note-RML131-144)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/BegramGladiator.JPG/250px-BegramGladiator.JPG)](/wiki/File:BegramGladiator.JPG)

Greco-Roman [gladiator](/wiki/Gladiator "Gladiator") on a glass vessel, Kapisa, 2nd century

> *Reges Bactrianorum legatos ad eum, amicitiae petendae causa, supplices miserunt*
> "The kings of the Bactrians sent supplicant ambassadors to him, to seek his friendship."[[136]](#cite_note-RML131-144)

Also in 138, according to [Aurelius Victor](/wiki/Aurelius_Victor "Aurelius Victor") (*Epitome*‚ XV, 4), and [Appian](/wiki/Appian "Appian") (*Praef.*, 7), [Antoninus Pius](/wiki/Antoninus_Pius "Antoninus Pius"), successor to Hadrian, received some Indian, Bactrian, and [Hyrcanian](/wiki/Hyrcanian "Hyrcanian") ambassadors.[[136]](#cite_note-RML131-144)

Some Kushan coins have an effigy of "Roma", suggesting a strong level of awareness and some level of diplomatic relations.[[136]](#cite_note-RML131-144)

The summer capital of the Kushan Empire in Kapisa has yielded a considerable amount of goods imported from the Roman Empire—in particular, various types of glassware. The Chinese described the presence of Roman goods in the Kushan realm:

> "Precious things from *Da Qin* [the Roman Empire] can be found there [in Tianzhu or Northwestern India], as well as fine [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton") cloths, fine wool carpets, perfumes of all sorts, [sugar candy](/wiki/Sugar_candy "Sugar candy"), [pepper](/wiki/Black_pepper "Black pepper"), [ginger](/wiki/Ginger "Ginger"), and black salt."

— Hou Hanshu[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHill200931-145)

[Parthamaspates of Parthia](/wiki/Parthamaspates_of_Parthia "Parthamaspates of Parthia"), a client of Rome and ruler of the kingdom of [Osroene](/wiki/Osroene "Osroene"), is known to have traded with the Kushan Empire, goods being sent by sea and through the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River").[[138]](#cite_note-146)

## Contacts with China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kushan_Empire&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Contacts with China")]

During the 1st and 2nd century AD, the Kushan Empire expanded militarily to the north, putting them at the center of the profitable Central Asian commerce. They are related to have collaborated militarily with the Chinese against nomadic incursion, particularly when they allied with the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") general [Ban Chao](/wiki/Ban_Chao "Ban Chao") against the Sogdians in 84, when the latter were trying to support a revolt by the king of Kashgar.[[139]](#cite_note-de_crespigny_2007_5-6-147) Around 85, they also assisted the Chinese general in an attack on [Turpan](/wiki/Turpan "Turpan"), east of the Tarim Basin.

Kushan coinage in China

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/KanishkaICoinFoundInKhotan.jpg/120px-KanishkaICoinFoundInKhotan.jpg)](/wiki/File:KanishkaICoinFoundInKhotan.jpg)

A bronze coin of Kanishka the Great found in [Khotan](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Khotan "Kingdom of Khotan"), [Tarim Basin](/wiki/Tarim_Basin "Tarim Basin").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Lead_Ingot_with_Characters%2C_Gansu_Provincial_Museum.jpg/250px-Lead_Ingot_with_Characters%2C_Gansu_Provincial_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lead_Ingot_with_Characters,_Gansu_Provincial_Museum.jpg)

[Eastern Han](/wiki/Eastern_Han "Eastern Han") inscriptions on lead ingot, using barbarous Greek alphabet in the style of the [Kushans](/wiki/Kushans "Kushans"), excavated in [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi"), 1st–2nd century AD. [Gansu Provincial Museum](/wiki/Gansu_Provincial_Museum "Gansu Provincial Museum").[[140]](#cite_note-148)[[141]](#cite_note-149)

In recognition for their support to the Chinese, the Kushans requested a Han princess, but were denied,[[139]](#cite_note-de_crespigny_2007_5-6-147)[[142]](#cite_note-torday1997-393-150) even after they had sent presents to the Chinese court. In retaliation, they marched on Ban Chao in 86 with a force of 70,000, but were defeated by a smaller Chinese force.[[139]](#cite_note-de_crespigny_2007_5-6-147)[[142]](#cite_note-torday1997-393-150) The Yuezhi retreated and paid tribute to the Chinese Empire during the reign of emperor [He of Han](/wiki/Emperor_He_of_Han_China "Emperor He of Han China") (89–106).

The Kushans are again recorded to have sent presents to the Chinese court in 158–159 during the reign of [Emperor Huan of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Huan_of_Han "Emperor Huan of Han").

Following these interactions, cultural exchanges further increased, and Kushan Buddhist missionaries, such as [Lokaksema](/wiki/Lokaksema_(Buddhist_monk) "Lokaksema (Buddhist monk)"), became active in the Chinese capital cities of [Luoyang](/wiki/Luoyang "Luoyang") and sometimes [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"), where they particularly distinguished themselves by their translation work. They were the first recorded promoters of Hinayana and Mahayana scriptures in China, greatly contributing to the [Silk Road transmission of Buddhism](/wiki/Silk_Road_transmission_of_Buddhism "Silk Road transmission of Buddhism").

## Decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kushan_Empire&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Decline")]

### Kushano-Sassanians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kushan_Empire&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Kushano-Sassanians")]

Main article: [Kushano-Sasanians](/wiki/Kushano-Sasanians "Kushano-Sasanians")

Sasanian control of the Western Kushans

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Hormizd_I_Kushanshah_on_the_Naqsh-e_Rustam_Bahram_II_panel.jpg/250px-Hormizd_I_Kushanshah_on_the_Naqsh-e_Rustam_Bahram_II_panel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hormizd_I_Kushanshah_on_the_Naqsh-e_Rustam_Bahram_II_panel.jpg)

[Hormizd I Kushanshah](/wiki/Hormizd_I_Kushanshah "Hormizd I Kushanshah") (AD 277–286), king of the [Indo-Sasanians](/wiki/Indo-Sasanians "Indo-Sasanians"), maintained [Sasanian](/wiki/Sasanian "Sasanian") rule in former Kushan territories of the northwest. [Naqsh-e Rustam](/wiki/Naqsh-e_Rustam "Naqsh-e Rustam") [Bahram II](/wiki/Bahram_II "Bahram II") panel.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Peroz_I_Kushanshah.jpg/250px-Peroz_I_Kushanshah.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peroz_I_Kushanshah.jpg)

The Kushano-Sasanians imitated the Kushans in some of their Bactrian coinage. Coin of Sasanian ruler [Peroz I Kushanshah](/wiki/Peroz_I_Kushanshah "Peroz I Kushanshah"), with [Bactrian](/wiki/Bactrian_script "Bactrian script") legend around "Peroz the Great Kushan King"

After the death of [Vasudeva I](/wiki/Vasudeva_I "Vasudeva I") in 225, the Kushan empire split into western and eastern halves. The Western Kushans (in Afghanistan) were soon subjugated by the Persian [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire") and lost [Sogdiana](/wiki/Sogdiana "Sogdiana"), [Bactria](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria"), and [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") to them. The Sassanian king [Shapur I](/wiki/Shapur_I "Shapur I") (240–270) claims in his [Naqsh-e Rostam](/wiki/Naqsh-e_Rostam "Naqsh-e Rostam") inscription possession of the territory of the Kushans (Kūšān šahr) as far as [Purushapura](/wiki/Purushapura "Purushapura") (modern Peshawar), suggesting he controlled [Bactria](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria") and areas as far as the [Hindu-Kush](/wiki/Hindu-Kush "Hindu-Kush") or even south of it:[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERezakhani2017b202–203-151)

> I, the Mazda-worshipping lord, Shapur, king of kings of Iran and An-Iran... (I) am the Master of the Domain of Iran (Ērānšahr) and possess the territory of Persis, Parthian... Hindestan, the Domain of the Kushan up to the limits of Paškabur and up to Kash, Sughd, and Chachestan.

— [Shapur I's inscription at the Ka'ba-ye Zartosht](/wiki/Shapur_I%27s_inscription_at_the_Ka%27ba-ye_Zartosht "Shapur I's inscription at the Ka'ba-ye Zartosht"), [Naqsh-e Rostam](/wiki/Naqsh-e_Rostam "Naqsh-e Rostam")[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERezakhani2017b202–203-151)

This is also confirmed by the [Rag-i-Bibi](/wiki/Rag-i-Bibi "Rag-i-Bibi") inscription in modern [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan").[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERezakhani2017b202–203-151)

The Sasanians deposed the Western dynasty and replaced them with Persian vassals known as the [Kushanshas](/wiki/Kushansha "Kushansha") (in [Bactrian](/wiki/Bactrian_language "Bactrian language") on their coinage: KΟÞANΟ ÞAΟ *Koshano Shao*)[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERezakhani2017b204-152) also called [Indo-Sasanians](/wiki/Indo-Sasanians "Indo-Sasanians") or [Kushano-Sasanians](/wiki/Kushano-Sasanians "Kushano-Sasanians"). The Kushano-Sasanians ultimately became very powerful under [Hormizd I Kushanshah](/wiki/Hormizd_I_Kushanshah "Hormizd I Kushanshah") (277–286) and rebelled against the Sasanian Empire, while continuing many aspects of the Kushan culture, visible in particular in their titulature and their coinage.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERezakhani2017b200–210-153)

### "Little Kushans" and Gupta suzerainty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kushan_Empire&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: \"Little Kushans\" and Gupta suzerainty")]

Gupta control over the Eastern Kushans

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Daivaputra_Shaahi_Shaahaanu_Shaahi.jpg/330px-Daivaputra_Shaahi_Shaahaanu_Shaahi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daivaputra_Shaahi_Shaahaanu_Shaahi.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Gupta_allahabad_de.svg/40px-Gupta_allahabad_de.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_de.svg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Gupta_allahabad_v.svg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_v.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_v.svg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Gupta_allahabad_pu.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_pu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_pu.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Gupta_ashoka_tr.jpg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_tr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_tr.jpg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Gupta_allahabad_ssa.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_ssa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_ssa.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Gupta_allahabad_hi.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_hi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_hi.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Gupta_allahabad_ssa.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_ssa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_ssa.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Gupta_allahabad_haa.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_haa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_haa.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Gupta_allahabad_nu.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_nu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_nu.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Gupta_allahabad_ssa.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_ssa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_ssa.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Gupta_allahabad_hi.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_hi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_hi.jpg)  
The expression *Devaputra Shāhi Shāhānu Shāhi* in Middle [Brahmi](/wiki/Brahmi "Brahmi") in the [Allahabad pillar](/wiki/Allahabad_pillar "Allahabad pillar") (Line 23), claimed by [Samudragupta](/wiki/Samudragupta "Samudragupta") to be under his dominion.[[146]](#cite_note-154)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Samudra_Gandhara_coin%2C_Punjabi_mint_Circa_CE_350-375.jpg/330px-Samudra_Gandhara_coin%2C_Punjabi_mint_Circa_CE_350-375.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samudra_Gandhara_coin,_Punjabi_mint_Circa_CE_350-375.jpg)

Coin minted in the [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab") area with the name "Samudra" ([![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Gupta_ashoka_s.svg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_s.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_s.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Gupta_allahabad_mu.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_mu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_mu.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Gupta_allahabad_dr.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_dr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_dr.jpg) *Sa-mu-dra*), thought to be the Gupta ruler [Samudragupta](/wiki/Samudragupta "Samudragupta"). These coins imitate those of the last Kushan ruler [Kipunada](/wiki/Kipunada "Kipunada"), and precede the coinage of the first [Kidarite Huns](/wiki/Kidarites "Kidarites") in northwestern India. Circa 350-375.[[147]](#cite_note-155)[[148]](#cite_note-156)

The Eastern Kushan kingdom, also known as the "Little Kushans", was based in the Punjab. Around 270 their territories on the Gangetic plain became independent under local dynasties such as the [Yaudheyas](/wiki/Yaudheyas "Yaudheyas"). Then in the mid-4th century they were subjugated by the Gupta Empire under [Samudragupta](/wiki/Samudragupta "Samudragupta").[[149]](#cite_note-HCCA166-157) In his inscription on the [Allahabad pillar](/wiki/Allahabad_pillar "Allahabad pillar") Samudragupta proclaims that the *Dēvaputra-Shāhi-Shāhānushāhi* (referring to the last Kushan rulers, being a deformation of the Kushan regnal titles *Devaputra*, *Shao* and *Shaonanoshao*: "Son of God, King, King of Kings") are now under his dominion, and that they were forced to "self-surrender, offering (their own) daughters in marriage and a request for the administration of their own districts and provinces".[[150]](#cite_note-158)[[149]](#cite_note-HCCA166-157)[[151]](#cite_note-159) This suggests that by the time of the Allahabad inscription the Kushans still ruled in [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab"), but under the suzerainty of the Gupta Emperor.[[149]](#cite_note-HCCA166-157)

Numismatics indicate that the coinage of the Eastern Kushans was much weakened: silver coinage was abandoned altogether, and gold coinage was debased. This suggests that the Eastern Kushans had lost their central trading role on the trade routes that supplied luxury goods and [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold").[[149]](#cite_note-HCCA166-157) Still, the [Buddhist art of Gandhara](/wiki/Greco-Buddhist_art "Greco-Buddhist art") continued to flourish, and cities such as [Sirsukh](/wiki/Sirsukh "Sirsukh") near [Taxila](/wiki/Taxila "Taxila") were established.[[149]](#cite_note-HCCA166-157)

### Sasanian, Kidarite and Alchon invasions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kushan_Empire&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Sasanian, Kidarite and Alchon invasions")]

Main articles: [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"), [Kidarites](/wiki/Kidarites "Kidarites"), and [Alchon Huns](/wiki/Alchon_Huns "Alchon Huns")

In the east around 350, Shapur II regained the upper hand against the [Kushano-Sasanian Kingdom](/wiki/Kushano-Sasanian_Kingdom "Kushano-Sasanian Kingdom") and took control of large territories in areas now known as [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), possibly as a consequence of the destruction of the Kushano-Sasanians by the [Chionites](/wiki/Chionites "Chionites").[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERezakhani2017a85-160) The Kushano-Sasanian still ruled in the north. Important finds of Sasanian coinage beyond the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") in the city of [Taxila](/wiki/Taxila "Taxila") only start with the reigns of Shapur II (r.309-379) and [Shapur III](/wiki/Shapur_III "Shapur III") (r.383-388), suggesting that the expansion of Sasanian control beyond the Indus was the result of the wars of Shapur II "with the [Chionites](/wiki/Kidarites "Kidarites") and Kushans" in 350-358 as described by [Ammianus Marcellinus](/wiki/Ammianus_Marcellinus "Ammianus Marcellinus").[[153]](#cite_note-AG-161) They probably maintained control until the rise of the [Kidarites](/wiki/Kidarites "Kidarites") under their ruler [Kidara](/wiki/Kidara "Kidara").[[153]](#cite_note-AG-161)

In 360 a [Kidarite Hun](/wiki/Kidarites "Kidarites") named [Kidara](/wiki/Kidara "Kidara") overthrew the [Kushano-Sasanians](/wiki/Kushano-Sasanians "Kushano-Sasanians") and remnants of the old Kushan dynasty, and established the [Kidarite Kingdom](/wiki/Kidarite_Kingdom "Kidarite Kingdom"). The Kushan style of Kidarite coins indicates they claimed Kushan heritage. The Kidarite seem to have been rather prosperous, although on a smaller scale than their Kushan predecessors. East of the [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab"), the former eastern territories of the Kushans were controlled by the mighty [Gupta Empire](/wiki/Gupta_Empire "Gupta Empire").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The remnants of Kushan culture under the Kidarites in the northwest were ultimately wiped out in the end of the 5th century by the invasions of the [Alchon Huns](/wiki/Alchon_Huns "Alchon Huns") (sometimes considered as a branch of the [Hephthalites](/wiki/Hephthalites "Hephthalites")), and later the [Nezak Huns](/wiki/Nezak_Huns "Nezak Huns").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Rulers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kushan_Empire&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Rulers")]

One of the most recent list of rulers with dates is as follows:[[154]](#cite_note-KKSK-162)

* [Heraios](/wiki/Heraios "Heraios") (c. 1 – 30), first king to call himself "Kushan" on his coinage [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Heraios_coin.jpg/60px-Heraios_coin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heraios_coin.jpg)

"Great Kushans";

* [Kujula Kadphises](/wiki/Kujula_Kadphises "Kujula Kadphises") (c. 50 – c. 90) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Kujula_Kadphises._Laureate_Julio-Claudian_style.jpg/60px-Kujula_Kadphises._Laureate_Julio-Claudian_style.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kujula_Kadphises._Laureate_Julio-Claudian_style.jpg)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kushan_Empire&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-The_Bactrian_2000_p._133_7-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-The_Bactrian_2000_p._133_7-1) The Kushans at first retained the [Greek language](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") for administrative purposes but soon began to use Bactrian. The Bactrian [Rabatak inscription](/wiki/Rabatak_inscription "Rabatak inscription") (discovered in 1993 and deciphered in 2000) records that the Kushan king [Kanishka the Great](/wiki/Kanishka_the_Great "Kanishka the Great") (c. 127 AD), discarded Greek (Ionian) as the language of administration and adopted Bactrian ("Arya language").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalk2001133-6)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** The Pali word *vaṃśa* (dynasty) affixed to *Gushana* (Kushana), i.e. Gushana-vaṃśa (Kushan dynasty) appears on a dedicatory inscription at [Manikiala stupa](/wiki/Mankiala "Mankiala").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosenfield1967[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidudnBkQhzHH4CpgPA7_7_&_8]-8)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-26)** It began about 127 CE.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalk2001121–136-23)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalk2004167–176-24)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHill200929,_33,_368–371-25)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-47)** For example, the 12th century historical chronicle from [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir"), the *[Rajatarangini](/wiki/Rajatarangini "Rajatarangini")*, describes the [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") Kushans as *Turushka* (तुरुष्क).
5. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-decipherment_65-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-decipherment_65-1) See also the analysis of [Sims-Williams & Cribb (1995–1996)](#CITEREFSims-WilliamsCribb1995–1996), specialists of the field, who had a central role in the decipherment.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-73)** For a translation of the full text of the Rabatak inscription see: [Mukherjee (1995)](#CITEREFMukherjee1995). This translation is quoted in: [Goyal (2005)](#CITEREFGoyal2005), p. 88.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-130)** Seated Buddha with inscription starting with [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Gupta_ashoka_m.svg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_m.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_m.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Gupta_ashoka_haa.jpg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_haa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_haa.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Gupta_allahabad_raa.jpg/20px-Gupta_allahabad_raa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_allahabad_raa.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Gupta_ashoka_j.svg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_j.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_j.svg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Gupta_ashoka_sya.svg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_sya.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_sya.svg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Gupta_ashoka_kaa.svg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_kaa.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_kaa.svg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Gupta_ashoka_nni.jpg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_nni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_nni.jpg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Gupta_ashoka_ssk.jpg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_ssk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_ssk.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Gupta_ashoka_sya.svg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_sya.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_sya.svg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Gupta_ashoka_sam.jpg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_sam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_sam.jpg)**𑁕** *Maharajasya Kanishkasya Sam 4* "Year 4 of the Great King Kanishka".
